title: As Fortunate as a Child Could Be
summary: Interviewed by Joe Mosnier at the University of Maryland, Baltimore County in July 2011, Freeman A. Hrabowski III opens on being born in Birmingham in 1950 to older, educated parents who had met at Alabama State in Montgomery and married in 1940, with Sixth Avenue Baptist Church, sister church to the one later bombed, at the center of everything, and a father whose family had lived on plantation land where the first Freeman was the first born after slavery.

title: Three Jobs Each
summary: He describes his father leaving teaching for the steel mill and the railroad, where he secretly did the reading and writing for white supervisors who had no high school education, and a household where both parents worked three jobs, his mother teaching by day, teaching GED at night, selling insurance, tutoring, and selling snowballs, in the Titusville middle-class neighborhood alongside Smithfield.

title: You'd Hear Bach in Church
summary: Hrabowski recounts a childhood spent always in church, in youth fellowship, choir, and Bible school, and the arrival of Pastor John T. Porter at thirty-two when he was twelve, an Alabama State and Morehouse man whose speaking he credits for his own, succeeding Reverend John Goodgame, with a pastor's wife who held graduate music degrees from NYU so that one heard Bach and piano lessons in a Southern Black church.

title: Polished by the Best of Whites
summary: He explains the class range within the middle class, from a few lawyers and physicians to people who worked for wealthy whites and traveled to Europe with their employers, mostly teachers, and his parents who constantly filled out financial-aid forms, pushed families to send children to college, and served as marriage counselors, in a city where his Polish surname Hrabowski was locally pronounced robust.

title: A Wall of Family Names
summary: Hrabowski tells how families put up their mortgages to build the new Sixth Avenue church when the congregation moved to Titusville to make way for the University of Alabama at Birmingham, leaving a wall of their names, and how the Black newspaper, the Birmingham World, ran a front-page story that the city had more Negroes owning their homes than any city in the country, the home being the great symbol of status.

title: You Don't Have Time to Be a Victim
summary: He recalls never having met anyone white as a child and only learning how distinctly hierarchical the two groups were when he peeled back the brown-paper cover of a second-grade book and found the name of a white school on it, and the teacher's answer, the same one his parents gave, that the book may be second rate but he was first rate and had no time to be a victim.

title: Fired for Organizing Teachers
summary: After a short break, Hrabowski describes a household where race and politics were discussed constantly, and his mother's pride at having been fired from a school system outside Birmingham in the late 1940s for trying to organize teachers to demand equal salaries, then hired into the plum Birmingham City system by Dr. C.R. Hayes, the director of Colored Schools.

title: You Must Be Twice as Good
summary: He recounts his father tutoring steel-mill men toward their GEDs and writing the Negro section of the company bulletin with advice on hard work and supporting families, and the central lesson both parents drove home, common among Black children in the Deep South, that the world is not fair so you must be twice as good, a philosophy of self-empowerment he still teaches his students.

title: Kiddieland We Could Not Enter
summary: Hrabowski notes his parents were proud members of the NAACP and the Alabama Christian Movement for Human Rights, and describes his first impressions of segregation, the Kiddieland amusement park with its Ferris wheel that Black children, including his friend Condi, had to pass by, the movie-house balcony, the side doors of restaurants, and the filthy separate water fountain that told them in subtle and unsubtle ways they were a caste system.

title: Almost Born in the Classroom
topic: A Precocious Boy and a Hidden Pregnancy
summary: He describes being a precocious only child of older parents, reading and starting school very young and nearly finishing first grade at four, hidden in the girls' restroom whenever the VIP superintendent Dr. Hayes, his godfather, visited, and his mother, who had hidden her pregnancy with loose clothing because pregnant women could not keep teaching, joking he was almost born in the classroom and born to teach.

title: The Best Liability You Ever Had
summary: Hrabowski recalls being kept busy teaching slow kids at five, and how, knowing police were not fair, his light-skinned father who could nearly pass and drove and talked too fast would be addressed politely by officers as Mr. Freeman A. Hrabowski until they saw the boy's kinky hair and the son's name on the license, after which the courtesy turned to free when you're a color boy, prompting his father's joke that he was the best liability he ever had.

title: Bombs Next Door
summary: He describes the February 1960 sit-ins as frightening because the targeted families, the Shuttlesworths and attorney Arthur Shores, were people his community knew in nearby Smithfield, so a bomb felt like it was next door, and how his church discussed ideas and books in youth fellowship led partly by Condoleezza Rice's father, developing critical thinking, until a principal forced to suspend demonstrating students had them read Thoreau's Civil Disobedience.

title: What's This Guy's Name? King
summary: Hrabowski tells how, sitting in the back of church doing math to be left alone, he was caught by a visiting man proposing that involving children in the march could let them attend the best schools in the city, which got his attention because he wanted to test how smart white children were, and how he asked the man's name and learned it was Reverend Martin Luther King, who was changing the vision from this is the way of the world to it does not have to be this way.

title: Mama, Dad, I've Got to Go
summary: He recalls wanting to believe King without being convinced change would come, referencing a current Smithsonian exhibition by his UMBC colleague Maurice Berger on the visual culture that let America see the fire hoses and dogs on television, and how after seeing children abused he told his parents he had to go, called them hypocrites when they refused, and was sent to his room before they relented after a sleepless night.

title: You're Our Treasure
topic: A Frightened Volunteer Chosen to Lead
summary: Hrabowski describes his parents praying with red eyes and explaining that they did not distrust him but the white people in the jail who did not care about Black children, telling him he was their treasure, his two-year-older cousin laughing that no dogs would bite him, and how, frightened but committed, the older boy was chosen to lead a group of children and keep them from reacting to police trained to provoke them.

title: Spat On at the Steps
summary: He recounts the training meetings at Sixth Avenue and being bused to the other church, practicing songs like Ain't Gonna Let Nobody Turn Me Around that gave the marching children strength, and how, knees shaking, he led them to kneel and pray on the steps for first-class citizenship until a red-faced man asked what he wanted, spat in his face, and threw the children into the paddy wagon bound for City Hall.

title: Reading the Bible to the Bad Boys
summary: Hrabowski describes five days in a juvenile facility among hardened boys, the teachers warned they would lose their jobs and families their homes if their children went to jail, so most of his fellow marchers came from the projects rather than the middle class, and how, a self-described fat math nerd unable to fight, he kept the bad boys back by reading aloud from the twenty-third Psalm, aided by one boy who had been in his mother's class.

title: What You Do This Day
topic: King at the Bars and Release
summary: He reflects that even children can make decisions that shape their lives and know right from wrong, and recounts the gloom of the dark cells, Dr. King coming with the parents who sang outside while he told the children through the bars that what they did that day would have an impact on children for generations, and the rush of appreciating freedom when he was released on the fifth day.

title: Mr. Bell Tied His Tie
summary: Back at Ullman High within a day, suspended and embarrassed, Hrabowski credits principal George C. Bell, the small mathematician with a booming voice who made him a mathematician and was the uncle of Alma Powell, and his counselor John W. Rice, father of Condoleezza Rice, recounting how Bell quietly arranged for suspended students to get homework at home until the federal courts ordered the children back to school.

title: Decency Among Whites
topic: The Court Order and a Fragile Hope
summary: He remembers the joy in church when news came, by a judge he believes was named Black, that the courts had ordered the children back to school, the relief of knowing some white person in power thought them not wrong, and the cautious hope of that September as the city fathers compromised with Dr. King, even though he had never seen a single Black person in any position of power downtown.

title: That Hand and That Ring
summary: Hrabowski recounts the Sunday in September when the pastor got a note at the pulpit, stopped preaching, and told them their sister church had been bombed, the first time anyone had seen church stop in the middle, and how he had said goodbye on Friday to fourteen-year-old Cynthia Wesley, who was killed, recalling the ring a father had given his daughter that morning, recognized on a severed hand, and nightmares that lasted years.

title: Life Is as Hard as Steel
summary: He describes the three coffins, the smallest holding eleven-year-old Denise McNair, and Dr. King's eulogy answering Black voices who blamed the jailed children for the bombing by taking responsibility and telling the grieving parents that life is as hard as steel, and the hope Hrabowski drew from seeing, for the first time, rabbis, priests, and Baptist ministers among the white mourners.

title: He Was Somebody's Child
summary: After a break, Hrabowski reflects, citing David Brooks's The Social Animal, that we are products of our childhood experiences, and how his parents worked to move the children past bitterness with the lesson that hatred eats on the inside and you hate the act, not the person, recalling that when Bull Connor died his mother wept and said he was somebody's child, a product of how he was raised, an enlightening and cathartic moment.

title: Content of Character
topic: A Worldview Built From the Pain
summary: He explains that the experience taught him King's lesson to judge people by the content of their character rather than race, a view reinforced by working with students from a hundred and fifty countries, and frames his life's work as helping people think well and act with one another, on a campus that lets people say what they really think, arguing that exposure to all kinds of people produces tolerance and isolation breeds stereotyping.

title: Invisible in an All-White Class
topic: A Summer in Massachusetts and Hampton
summary: Hrabowski recounts a summer in Massachusetts at thirteen in an all-white eleventh-grade class whose rich, rigorous education he loved but where no teacher ever called on him, a feeling of being invisible he likens to Ralph Ellison's Invisible Man, and his enrollment in 1966 at Hampton Institute, chosen for its tie to Booker T. Washington, where caring white teachers in math and science first showed him many decent white people.

title: I'm Going to Marry Her One Day
summary: He describes taking calculus at the University of Alabama at Birmingham after his freshman year and discovering that, though the best student in his segregated school, he was less prepared than Black students from Northern prep and integrated schools, including the better-prepared young woman with Latin whom he vowed to marry and did four years later, and learning that the white Alabama students were poorly prepared too, so everyone had suffered from segregation.

title: A PhD Scientist for Mayor
summary: Hrabowski describes watching Birmingham change through periodic visits and his home church, friends going to formerly white universities, and his pride when Richard Arrington Jr., a PhD scientist, became mayor, even as the influx of educated people drawn by the University of Alabama at Birmingham was matched by the arrival of the same Black-on-black violence he had assumed belonged only to Northern cities.

title: Teachers Who Did Not Want You There
summary: He recounts the demographic shift as families moved to the suburbs and Birmingham city schools became increasingly Black, and explains that his mother kept him out of integrated schools like Ramsay and Phillips in the tenth grade because she did not trust teachers who did not want him there, recalling the violence and thrown objects that met his friends during desegregation and her conviction that unwelcoming teachers cannot give a child the best education.

title: Why the Adverb?
topic: From a Topology Grade to a Mission
summary: Hrabowski tells how at the University of Illinois graduate school, with no Black faculty, a professor wrote you did surprisingly well on his A-minus topology test, prompting the English-minded student to ask why the adverb and to refuse, as in second grade, to be defined as a victim, and how, finding no predominantly white university that produced even ten Black science doctorates a year, he met the Jewish philanthropist Robert E. Meyerhoff and married their concerns into a program.

title: It Does Not Have to Be This Way
summary: He describes defining excelling as earning PhDs and MD-PhDs from the best universities, and the program since 1989 producing hundreds of such graduates now on the medical faculties of Harvard, Stanford, Duke, Hopkins, and Michigan, moving beyond the lone exception of a Tuskegee or George Washington Carver, and connects the achievement to the childhood lesson that just because it is this way does not mean it has to be, the same boy who could not sit with whites becoming president of a largely white university.

title: What the Movement Was Really About
summary: Closing, Hrabowski contrasts the roughly two percent of African Americans who had completed college in 1963 with the seventeen to twenty percent today, and argues the civil rights movement was about opportunity but also about families and communities that focus on education, values, and dreams, warning that without that focus the underclass persists, that race still matters alongside poor white children, and that teaching every child to read well decides whether the future is bright.
